title: Strong brand and benefits, but slowed down by heavy bureaucracy
body: Strong brand and benefits, but slowed down by heavy bureaucracy
pros: Strong brand reputation, good compensation and benefits, exposure to large-scale financial data and well-established ML governance practices, generally reasonable work-life balance, and opportunities to collaborate with talented colleagues across different teams.
cons: Heavy bureaucracy slows down decision making, multiple approval layers make experimentation difficult, innovation is limited by strict governance processes, organizational silos reduce visibility into real business impact, and career growth can depend heavily on team and manager rather than a clear company-wide path.

title: Still the best company i've ever worked for
body: Still the best company i've ever worked for
pros: American Express genuinely cares about people in a way I rarely see in large companies. In many places (especially in the US) it can feel like employees are disposable. At Amex itâs different: leadership consistently prioritizes stability, long-term thinking, and treating people with respect. Steve Squeri has repeatedly emphasized a people-first mindset and avoiding âeasyâ cuts, and that tone clearly trickles down through the org. The culture is strong, teams are collaborative, and your opinion is actively sought and taken seriously (surveys, feedback loops, retros). When the company performs well, employees share in it through meaningful bonuses and benefits. Iâm genuinely proud to work here, which is not something I say easily about a multi-billion company.
cons: Because itâs a large enterprise, things can move slowly. Decision-making can involve multiple stakeholders, and process/compliance can add overhead. Some systems are understandably complex due to scale, legacy, and risk management, so you sometimes need patience and persistence to get change shipped. If you like very fast startup-style execution with minimal process, this may feel heavy at times.

Employees benefit from a comprehensive package of health and dental insurance, a private pension scheme via salary sacrifice, smart spending discounts, and initiatives such as cycleâtoâwork. These perks provide both professional enrichment and personal wellbeing support, making the overall benefits package one of the stronger aspects of the role.
cons: The role often involves long and demanding shifts, frequent rotations, and constant changes to service models. These shifts in structure can create confusion for clients and make it difficult for employees to maintain accountability for their own work. Over time, this can diminish enthusiasm and focus for the role, which ideally should be carried out with consistency, care, and attention to detail.

title: Great Benefits, Nice Employees, Flexible
pros: I had a great experience working at American Express. The company offered a flexible hybrid work model that made it easy to balance my professional and personal life. The benefits were strong, including generous PTO, paid parental leave, and daily meal credits that employees could use for lunch. My coworkers were consistently kind and supportive, and the culture emphasized doing meaningful work while still valuing time with family. Most of the team was in their late twenties to thirties, and people were focused, collaborative, and committed to doing their jobs well without unnecessary stress. Overall, Amex provided a positive, balanced work environment with great people and solid benefits.
cons: On the downside, the compensation could have been more competitive for the New York market. After COVID, leadership also began pushing for more in-office days, and the expectations around hybrid work werenât always clear. Many of the responsibilities could have been handled fully remotely, but upper management preferred employees to be on-site more often, which created some uncertainty for those who valued flexible or remote arrangements.

title: Great Earning Potential and Flexibility, but Challenging Sales Cycles
body: Great Earning Potential and Flexibility, but Challenging Sales Cycles
pros: Pros: Uncapped commissions with strong earning potential â hitting 100% to target pays just over $100K (before tax), and 150% to target can reach around $160K total. Base salary + commission structure is fair and motivating for high performers. Stock options and profit-sharing match provide excellent long-term incentives. Comprehensive benefits package (health, dental, vision, etc.). Flexible hybrid schedule: typically 3 days in office, 2 from home, with the opportunity to earn more WFH days based on performance. Strong brand reputation and supportive peers who make collaboration enjoyable. Great culture. Range of age groups but has lots of young adults and lots of willingness to help one another.
cons: Cons: Full end-to-end sales cycle â from prospecting to closing â can be demanding and time-intensive. Economic conditions can directly impact credit approvals and spending limits, making it harder to hit goals in slower markets. Performance-driven environment; not ideal for those who prefer a steady pace or dislike rejection. Targets always increasing so technically always having to work harder for same commission payout (based on percentage to target).

title: Training is subpar
pros: Insurance, benefits, 401k, $15.00 daily meal allotment when working in office, culture
cons: The claims department is so small, they don't have a training protocol. There are no training materials. It is run by one person who has been at the company for more than 10 years, but if that person is out or not available, there are no back-ups. You mainly learn when you are on the floor and that is also when your metrics count to be able to keep your job. So you have to pay good attention in class. If you have questions regarding the phone system - good luck. They run metrics like a call center in terms of claims per hour, because you have to be in "ready" status. They have a terrible time reacting to the fire claims of Jan 2025. Everything is handled reactively and not proactively.

title: 3.5 stars
pros: Very structured. Company has great benefits.
cons: The management in the Travel Department are not from Travel Sales Departments and absolutely have no clue how to set the proper atmosphere. It makes work dull and boring. They will try not to answer how much your commissions can be for as long as they possibly can. They'll even mislead you on pay expectations throughout the hiring and training process. In the end you'll come to realize they are not trying to payout much in commissions and they pocket all the commissions paid on travel that CMs purchase. Those commissions can be more than you're paid monthly and you DO NOT get a percentage of it. Needless to say... People leave on a regular basis if they don't value the company. If they do value the company they quickly leave the department. Travel Sales Professionals SAY AWAY FROM Travel Consultant positions at American Express.

title: Decent overall, though management
pros: Good PTO Good healthcare Worked fully remote for the time I was there. Work life balance was decent.
cons: Management can be pretty hit or miss. As you go higher up the chain the more detached management is from the realities of the job. Pretty hands off management style which can certainly be good most of the time if you are responsible, but it is an issue when it comes time for ratings. Management did not provide input areas for improvement or excellence when asked. All new positions it seems are returning to office. When I was hired during the pandemic we all were assured that we would remain remote. New senior leadership decided instead to eliminate the team as a whole and make everyone reapply to force a return to office under new contracts.

pros: Great colleagues who are talented and supportive. Interesting projects with strong company brand and resources. Compensation and benefits are fantastic.
cons: Leadership engagement on our team is severely lacking. Over the course of the past year, I havenât had a single 1:1 meeting with my leader. Some team members didnât even receive a midyear review. Slack messages and Outlook emails often go unread or unanswered, making it difficult to get timely feedback and clarity. Our leader regularly doesnât show up to scheduled and accepted meetings, which disrupts team coordination and morale.

title: Great experience to develop skills and work in a professional environment
body: Great experience to develop skills and work in a professional environment
pros: Strong brand name and global reputation; looks great on your CV. Comprehensive training that helps you develop valuable skills in customer service, risk management, and decision-making. Supportive and professional work culture; most managers are approachable and genuinely invested in employee development. Opportunities to learn about the financial services industry and work with international markets (I supported US-based customers from abroad). Good benefits package and a structured work environment.
cons: The work can be repetitive and metrics-driven; constant focus on call quality, handling time, and compliance can feel intense at times. Limited opportunities for quick career progression in certain teams unless you're willing to move laterally or relocate. Schedules can be rigid, with some shifts that may not suit everyone.

title: 75% Escalations
pros: Good pay if you make your bonus. Good benefits. Generous PTO. Virtual position. Profit share. Centurion Directors/Senior leadership are great. Building strong relationships with some clients.
cons: Bonuses vary based on constantly changing metrics-can be 5k or higher or $0, nothing, IMO held accountable in large part for metrics outside your control. i.e you get assigned a not so great portfolio or under-staffed in pod, causing service levels to go down, more mistakes and card member dissatisfaction. Stressful-high demand, sometimes verbally abusive clients. Limited span of control. Have to clean up messes for other peopleâs mistakes. IMO job is more like a Membership Executive complaint department instead of a relationship manager. In my opinion, MEs are not empowered in their pod. Responsibility but no authority with Membership Managers.

title: Save your mental health
pros: Comprehensive healthcare and dental benefits
cons: Travel & Lifestyle department is treated differently (badly) from any other departments of Amex. 0 to non opportunities to progress/change career path. Call centre environment. Hired people under false representation. For example, you are hired as a Relationship Manager/Leader with the basic salary but promised commissions /bonuses of 30-50% your base, in fact once you joined they changes the rules and removed commissions structure. Constant job title changes, high turnover, constant team moves. Threats of redundancy and then backtracking. Adding more changes to the role just to see what the breaking point is for people. No HR, everything is a bot or chat. KPIs are stupid and make no sense. After working for 4 years, my mental health was completely destroyed. Just avoid!
